description: This course is in progress, but allowing our users to preview the work in progress! As we add material we will update the course regularly. Expect the case list to expand soon, followed by an introductory video. We will update the course name when the course is complete! Happy learning.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Contrast-enhanced CT Neck:

  • Enlarged palatine (T) tonsils, with a striated 'tigroid' enhancement pattern.
  • Small right peritonsillar hypoattenuation (P), representative of a small abscess, measuring 1.8 cm.

Make sure to rule out direct spread of infection into the adjacent neck spaces: parapharyngeal, retropharyngeal, and submandibular spaces.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Tonsillitis refers to inflammation of the palatine tonsils, which is a clinical diagnosis and often treated medically. We image to diagnose and look for complications/spread.

Imaging Findings:

Acutely inflamed palatine tonsils are enlarged and exhibit a striated 'tigroid' pattern.

  • This pattern can only be seen on contrast-enhanced studies and is due to the crypt-based architecture of the tonsils, accentuated by vascular congestion, edema, and localized exudate along those crypts.

If left untreated, can develop an abscess deep to the tonsil, between the tonsillar capsule and the superior constrictor muscle, also known as 'peritonsillar abscess.'

  • This potential space is situated between the tonsil and the parapharyngeal space, not quite within the parapharyngeal space. Look for a hypoattenuating collection, which may or may not exhibit peripheral enhancement, deep to the tonsil, but displaces the parapharyngeal space laterally.

Much less commonly, tonsilitis can progress into an abscess that is confined within the tonsil, also known as 'tonsillar abscess' (sometimes 'intratonsillar abscess')

  • Look for a well-defined fluid collection confined within an enlarged tonsil.

Pearls:

  • Report proximity to the internal carotid artery and any medialization of ICA to guide safe drainage
  • Note any spread to parapharyngeal, retropharyngeal spaces (deep neck infection), which impacts urgency and management
  • Comment on airway narrowing if present
  • Phlegmonous change alone can mimic abscess but is less well defined

Management:

If the abscess measures less than 2 cm and is confined within one neck space, clinicians may elect to treat this with an empiric trial of antibiotic therapy. For abscesses larger than 2 cm, clinicians are more inclined to attempt transoral incision and drainage. Exact thresholds may vary.

If further left untreated, infection can spread into the parapharygeal space, then retropharyngeal or submandibular space.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Bilateral tonsillitis with 'kissing tonsils' and peritonsillar abscess

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Enlarged tonsils with a striated 'tigroid' pattern
  • The tonsils are touching at midline, also known as 'kissing tonsils'
  • Small 1.3 cm peripherally enhancing fluid collection in the right peritonsillar space, indicative of abscess
  • Stranding, possibly phlegmon, in the right parapharyngeal space, (compare to the left)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
When the tonsils are so enlarged in acute tonsillitis that they are touching in the midline, they are referred to as 'kissing tonsils'.

Report on the degree of airway narrowing, and be sure to raise the possibility of airway compromise in the impression of your report!


Similar to our previous case, note:

  • Striated heterogeneous enhancement with acute inflammation
  • Look for complications -- i.e. peritonsillar phlegmon, peritonsillar abscess, intratonsillar abscess, and deep extension of infection (parapharyngeal, retropharyngeal, danger space).


Next Level Trivia:

  • Grisel's Syndrome - rare, in paeds: associated C1-2 rotatory subluxation due to inflammatory ligamentous laxity after infection. Inciting infection could be tonsillitis, deep neck infection, otitis, or other. They present with painful torticollis.

cases.clinicalHistory: Four-day history of sore throat, limited neck range of motion, occasional fevers

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large parapharyngeal abscess (A) with Peripheral enhancement (E) -- the hallmark of a mature drainable collection
  • Extension of abscess and stranding into the vascular space -- a red flag to look for vascular complications
  • Circumferential wall thickening of the internal carotid artery -- indicating inflammation (infectious arteritis) and risk for vascular complications
  • Compressed left internal jugular vein -- due to mass effect. Look closely for thrombus (none here)
  • Retropharyngeal reactive effusion (R) -- no abscess. Look closely for spread to mediastinum (not present here)
  • Reactive adenopathy
  • Ossified triticeous cartilage (O) -- normal variant (not a foreign body)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
What is a parapharyngeal abscess?

  • Deep neck infection in the parapharyngeal space (lateral to pharyngeal constrictor; contains carotid sheath). It often arises from tonsillitis/PTA, odontogenic or masticator space infection, or lymphatic spread from upper airway mucosa
  • It can communicate with adjacent spaces (retropharyngeal, masticator, submandibular) and threaten airway and great vessels; early CT with contrast is key

Typical microbiology and cause

  • Polymicrobial aerobes/anaerobes: common pathogens include Strep anginosus group, Fusobacterium spp., and oral streptococci; odontogenic sources are frequent in adults
  • Blood cultures may be negative; abscess cultures usually positive. Empiric therapy should cover oral streptococci, anaerobes, and possible S. aureus, then tailor to culture

Complications to anticipate and actively exclude

  • Airway compromise, laryngeal edema.
  • Vascular: IJV thrombosis (Lemierre syndrome), ICA arteritis/pseudoaneurysm/erosion, septic emboli.
  • Fascial spread: retropharyngeal “danger space” to mediastinum; chest sepsis/empyaema

What to report:

  • Size, location, and compartments involved; presence of gas.
  • Rim-enhancing collection vs phlegmon; clear statement if “drainable.”
  • Relationship to carotid sheath: distance to ICA for transoral approach; note medialized/high-riding carotid variants (i.e. vascular variants that are important to know before draining)
  • Vascular findings: ICA wall thickening/irregularity, enhancement defects; IJV patency, filling defects, or compression.
  • Retropharyngeal/danger space fluid and inferior extension toward mediastinum.
  • Lymph nodes (reactive vs necrotic), airway narrowing, laryngeal edema.

Management implications (imaging-driven)

  • Airway first; urgent ENT consult. Large rim-enhancing collections with carotid sheath involvement typically require drainage plus broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., ampicillin–sulbactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole), adapted to culture
  • Suspected IJV thrombosis or ICA involvement warrants neck venous phase CT/CTV and often chest imaging; consider anticoagulation if Lemierre, per multidisciplinary decision
  • Repeat imaging if clinical non-response or to reassess vascular complications.

Practical pearls

  • Not all fluid is abscess: reactive retropharyngeal effusion lacks thick rim enhancement
  • Carotid sheath signs (ICA mural thickening, perivascular fat stranding, IJV non-opacification) upgrade urgency—state explicitly in impression
  • Odontogenic pathway commonly via masticator space—trace dental roots and masticator compartments on CT to find the source
  • Document safe drainage corridors and carotid proximity for the operator

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Small fluid collection in the right medial pterygoid muscle
  • Subperiosteal abscess deep to the mandible. This involves periosteal lift along mandibular cortex -- look for cortical breach/early osteomyelitis and the nearest diseased tooth (see below).
  • Stranding within the right masticator space (1,2)
  • Thickening of the right platysma
  • Stranding/inflammation within the right submandibular space (1,2)
  • Multiple reactive cervical nodes (1,2) -- expected, but look for necrotic areas (not present here)
  • Stranding throughout the right anterior neck
  • Trace apical lucency deep to a right mandibular molar tooth (1,2) -- the source of infection

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is an odontogenic masticator space abscess -- i.e. caused by tooth infection. Masticator space includes mandible/ramus, TMJ, and muscles of mastication/chewing (masseter, pterygoids, temporalis); and carries V3 (mandibular) nerve.

These often present with trismus (can't open mouth), jaw pain, swelling. CT/MRI are helpful as difficult to examine this region.

Why it matters (complications)

  • Infection can spread to submandibular, parapharyngeal spaces and can impact airway
  • Suprazygomatic extension (above zygoma) can be missed.
  • Cavernous sinus/intracranial spread is rare
  • Medial masticator abscesses tend to spread early to the parapharyngeal space/soft palate; submasseteric space is a key pathway laterally

What to put in your report (ESSENTIALS)

  • Source tooth and any mandibular cortical breakthrough/periosteal reaction (look for osteomyelitis)
  • Exact compartments: medial vs lateral masticator; presence/size of rim-enhancing collection; gas.
  • Extra-spatial spread: submandibular, parapharyngeal, suprazygomatic temporal extension.
  • Airway narrowing or parapharyngeal space involvement (upgrade urgency).
  • Operative roadmap: subperiosteal vs intramuscular, proximity to mandibular canal; suprazygomatic extension may need separate drainage.

Management implications

  • Large/rim-enhancing collections → drainage plus broad-spectrum odontogenic coverage; small collections may be observed if responding clinically
  • Choose approach based on location: intraoral for submasseteric/medial pterygoid common; alternatives exist beyond scope here.
  • Reimage if persistent fever/trismus or progression to parapharyngeal/temporal spaces.

Pearls

  • Trismus out of proportion to swelling → think pterygoid abscess, not TMJ
  • Medial masticator abscess + parapharyngeal extension is common; say it clearly in the impression
  • Always look above the zygoma for temporal extension; it may need a second drain tract

cases.clinicalHistory: 2 days left neck pain and mass?

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Unilocular cystic structure with thin peripheral rim of enhancement measuring 3 cm.
  • It sits near angle of mandible: anterior to internal jugular vein (compressed), lateral to common carotid, and medial to SCM (sternocleidomastoid), posterior to submandibular.
  • Surrounding stranding (also see inferior)
  • Mild mass effect on submandibular gland just anteiror with some mild secondary inflammation/edema
  • Clustered prominent left level II and III nodes (scroll), likely reactive.

Other Findings:

  • Focal opacification of right posterior ethmoid sinus without expansion or bony changes.

IMPRESSION:

3 cm left neck abscess, likely an infected second branchial cleft cyst type 2 given age and location.

Differential includes infected level II node. Close clinical follow-up and imaging after resolution of the acute phase is suggested.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is an infected second branchial cleft cyst - a classic exam question. Learn about branchial cleft cyst typical locations, complications, and the critical differentials.


Branchial cleft cysts are congenital epithelial cysts, often presenting in younger patients with fluctuant neck mass. Most are at 2nd branchial cleft (90%). On imaging, they are well-circumscribed unilocular cysts, and with infection show thick enhancing rim with surrounding stranding (like our case).

Typical Locations are Important to Know:

  • 1st branchial cleft: Peri parotid / near EAC
  • 2nd (most common): classic = anteromedial to SCM, posterior to submandibular, lateral to carotid. There are actually 4 types (Bailey types) of second branchial cleft cysts, with type 2 being most common (like this case).
  • 3rd - lower lateral neck near thyroid, tract often extends to pyriform sinus
  • 4th - left low neck, paratracheal region, often involving thyroid and tracheoesophageal groove. Can have tract from inferior pyriform sinus as well.

Complications:

  • Infection / abscess formation - wall thickening, surrounding stranding, compress adjacent structures (enlarge)
  • Hemorrhage (also can enlarge)
  • Malignancy extremely rare

Classic Ddx to consider:

  • **Cystic / Necrotic metastatic node - i.e. HPV-related SCC. Look for a mucosal primary. This is the key adult mimic that drives need for close follow-up.
  • Suppurative lymphadenitis (infection)
  • Lymphatic malformation (usually multiseptated, trans-spatial, etc)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Edema of the right palatine tonsil, indicative of tonsillitis
  • Multifocal hypoattenuating collections at the lateral aspect of the right palatine tonsil, indicative peritonsillar abscesses
  • Thrombosis of the common facial vein
  • Thrombosis of the right internal jugular vein
  • Multifocal nodules, consolidation, and ground glass within the lungs

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Lemierre Syndrome is septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein (IJV) following an oropharyngeal infection, with potential for septic embolization. It predominantly affects young, previously healthy adults, and the classic causative organism is Fusobacterium necrophorum, a normal oral flora anaerobe that becomes dangerous when infection spreads beyond the pharynx.

Think of it as a sinister progression: tonsillitis or a peritonsillar abscess → spread into the adjacent deep neck spaces → involvement of the internal jugular vein → septic IJV thrombophlebitis. This vascular step is the defining event of the syndrome.

CT Neck: What to look for

  • Peritonsillar or parapharyngeal rim-enhancing fluid collection (abscess) — the initiating source
  • Fat stranding tracking along the carotid sheath
  • Filling defect within the IJV on post-contrast images. Vein may appear expanded if acute, with a hypoattenuating filling defect. This is the key finding.
  • The common facial vein (which drains into the IJV) is often involved early, as in this case
  • Surrounding soft tissue oedema along the course of the IJV

A critical technical point: imaging phase matters

  • IJV thrombosis must be confirmed on a true venous phase acquisition.
  • On late arterial phase imaging, the IJV may not yet be fully opacified, leading to mixing artifact that can mimic a filling defect and falsely suggest thrombosis. If it's mixing artifact, the pseudo-filling defect will resolve on venous phase as contrast fills the vein.
  • Always correlate with the venous phase before calling IJV thrombosis.

Septic pulmonary emboli: important nuance

  • Infected thrombus can embolize to the lungs in Lemierre syndrome, producing peripheral bilateral nodules, often with cavitation
  • However, pulmonary involvement is not a universal or required feature. The diagnosis rests on the combination of an oropharyngeal source and IJV thrombosis.

Why it matters?

  • IJV thrombosis is easy to miss if the neck vessels are not actively interrogated on post-contrast CT
  • Recognizing and connecting the abscess to the IJV filling defect is the radiologist's critical contribution

Always confirm thrombosis on venous phase to avoid a false positive diagnosis with real clinical consequences. 

Management:

  • Prolonged IV antibiotics are the mainstay (+/- source control = drain collections)
  • Anticoagulation evidence is mixed - but considered case-by-case

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Skin thickening
  • Subcutaneous stranding
  • Intramuscular phlegmon
  • No mature fluid collection / abscess

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
What to look for on CT:

  • Cellulitis: skin thickening, fat stranding/reticulation, asymmetric, septal enhancement favors infection over bland edema
  • Phlegmon: ill-defined, enhancing inflamed soft tissue/muscle, without a mature non-enhancing core (like in our case).
  • Abscess: central low attenuation with peripheral/rim enhancement, possible gas; often with positive mass effect.
  • Necrotizing fasciitis: soft-tissue gas, deep fascial thickening (>3 mm), fluid tracking along deep/intermuscular fascia, nonenhancing fascia; gas is highly specific but not required

cases.clinicalHistory: Acute neck pain, fever, and dysphagia
cases.diagnosisTitle: Calcific Tendonitis of the Longus Colli

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Retropharyngeal effusion (also see sagittal images) without peripheral enhancement
  • Amorphous calcification within the superior fibers of the longus colli muscle (also see axial and coronal images), just inferior to anterior arch of C1
  • No adenopathy
  • Normal vertebra and intervertebral discs

Findings are in keeping with calcific tendinitis of the longus colli.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Calcific tendinitis of the longus colli = calcium hydroxyapatite crystals deposited in the tendon of the longus colli muscle in the neck.

This triggers a short-lived aseptic inflammation (not infection) causing acute neck pain/stiffness and often painful swallowing. It usually resolves with NSAIDs/steroids over days–weeks. It is important to differentiate from retropharyngeal infection/abscess.

Where to look

  • Classic site: Superior oblique tendon of longus colli, just inferior to the anterior arch of C1 (C1–C2 level) -- i.e. scroll down from C1
  • On CT (best test): look for amorphous calcification at C1–C2 and sheet-like retropharyngeal/prevertebral edema/effusion that tapers superiorly/inferiorly and has no rim enhancement (helps avoid calling abscess)
  • On radiograph: may see prevertebral soft tissue swelling and subtle calcification anterior to C1–C2
  • MRI: shows prevertebral edema/effusion, but often misses the calcium—use CT to confirm

Pearl to avoid pitfalls

  • Think of it when you see the triad: acute neck pain + stiffness + odynophagia, mild inflammatory labs, and CT with C1–C2 tendon calc plus non–rim-enhancing retropharyngeal fluid.
  • Call it calcific tendinitis to prevent unnecessary antibiotics or drainage

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Severely edematous and thickened epiglottis
  • Severely edematous and thickened supraglottic larynx (aryepiglottic folds)
  • Subcutaneous inflammation and platysma thickening
  • Severe airway narrowing

Findings are in keeping with Epiglottitis.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Acute epiglottitis (supraglottitis) = infection/inflammation of the epiglottis and nearby supraglottic larynx tissues (aryepiglottic folds, arytenoids). Swelling can rapidly narrow the airway and become life-threatening.

Clinical:

  • Though commonly considered a pediatric disease, often caused by H flu, in the post-vaccine era, Adults now predominate (and Group A Strep)
  • Diagnosis is primarily clinical/laryngoscopic; imaging must never delay airway management.
  • Still, you may rarely see this on imaging

Key radiographic signs (lateral neck radiograph)

  • Thumb sign: bulbous thickened epiglottis (normally thin); may be accompanied by thickened aryepiglottic folds and ballooned hypopharynx. In adults, epiglottis >8 mm; aryepiglottic folds >7 mm are suggestive
  • Vallecula sign: loss of normal vallecular air space
  • Sensitivity is variable; normal film does not exclude epiglottitis. Use as adjunct when laryngoscopy not immediately available.

CT findings to know

  • Diffuse or focal thickening/edema of the epiglottis; can involve aryepiglottic folds and arytenoids. Supraglottic mucosal enhancement/stranding; airway narrowing
  • Distribution: edema typically does not extend below the true cords; initial thickening may be anterior (lingual) surface of epiglottis
  • Complications: look for an epiglottic abscess (rim-enhancing collection), and spread to deep neck spaces (parapharyngeal/retropharyngeal), which escalates airway risk and may alter management
  • Caveat: CT is not required for diagnosis and should not delay airway stabilization

cases.clinicalHistory: C/o left neck pain and tenderness

data.text: Mild luminal narrowing of the left CCA [comapre with contrlateral side]

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Asymmetric soft tissue thickening involving the wall and perivascular tissue surrounding the left common carotid artery, extending to the carotid bifurcation
  • Compare left (abnormal) and right (normal)
  • Slight luminal narrowing of the left common carotid artery secondary to wall thickening
  • Airway widely patent

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
TIPIC Syndrome (Transient Perivascular Inflammation of the Carotid artery), also called carotidynia, is a benign, self-limiting inflammatory condition causing focal perivascular soft tissue thickening around the carotid artery. It presents with unilateral neck pain and tenderness overlying the carotid, and is a diagnosis made on imaging.

CT/MRI appearances:

  • Eccentric perivascular soft tissue thickening around the carotid — classically at the bifurcation
  • Enhancement of perivascular tissue on post-contrast imaging
  • Mild luminal narrowing may be present
  • No collection, no adenopathy
  • On MRI — T2 hyperintensity in perivascular tissue confirms active inflammation. MRI is preferred when CT is equivocal.

Critical differentials to exclude

  • Dissection: crescentic mural hematoma on T1 fat-sat MRI with calibre of vessel larger but luminal narrowing (an intimal flap less common in neck)
  • Vasculitis: more diffuse, often involves multiple vessels
  • Malignant adenopathy: discrete nodal architecture encasing the carotid, or more mass-like infiltrative tissue
  • Deep neck infection: organized collection, space involvement.

TIPIC is a diagnosis of exclusion. This means it can only be diagnosed once other etiologies have been considered and excluded on imaging and clinically.

TIPIC syndrome is self-limiting — symptoms typically resolve within weeks with NSAIDs or conservative management. Imaging findings resolve on follow-up studies. No surgical or interventional treatment is required. This makes accurate consideration on imaging impactful as it can help spare the patient from unnecessary vascular intervention, biopsy, or prolonged investigation.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings:
teachingSections.html:
• Large hypervascular, avidly enhancing mass centred in the posterior nasal cavity / nasopharynx, measuring 5.6 x 5.4 x 4.5 cm
  • Lateral extension into and expansion of the left sphenopalatine fossa and pterygopalatine fossa - the classic spread pattern of JNA
  • Superior extension with destruction of the floor of the sphenoid sinus; floor of sella tursica intact
  • Involvement of the left inferior orbital fissure and vidian canal
  • Anterior extension destroying the posterior nasal septum, turbinates, and posteromedial maxillary sinus walls
  • Mass inferiorly displacing the soft palate
  • Prominent bilateral internal maxillary arteries - dominant feeding vessels

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Juvenile Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma (JNA) is a benign but locally aggressive, highly vascular tumour of the nasopharynx, occurring almost exclusively in adolescent and young adult males. Despite being histologically benign, it destroys bone and can extend intracranially, as in this case.

It classically originates near the sphenopalatine foramen and expands along paths of least resistance. Pterygopalatine fossa widening is the hallmark. Look for bowing and expansion of its walls, which is near pathognomonic for JNA.

CT appearances:

  • Avidly enhancing hypervascular mass
  • Bone remodeling and/or destruction depending on growth rate and direction
  • Prominent feeding vessels - typically the internal maxillary artery and ascending pharyngeal artery, identifiable on CTA as in this case

MRI:

CT defines bony destruction and feeding vessels, MRI is the modality of choice for characterizing soft tissue extent, intracranial involvement, and cavernous sinus invasion -- all of which directly impact surgical planning.

  • T1 and T2 intermediate / heterogeneous signal
  • The classic sign = flow voids: Multiple internal flow voids on T2 representing high-flow vascular channels within the tumour. This is the MRI hallmark of JNA and reflects its angiofibroma nature.
  • A nasopharyngeal mass with flow voids in a young male = think JNA first
  • Avid enhancement, similar to CT
  • MRI best defines cavernous sinus involvement and intracranial extension — always look here on coronal post-contrast T1

Spread routes to systematically report:

  • Pterygopalatine fossa → orbit (inferior orbital fissure), middle cranial fossa, infratemporal fossa
  • Vidian canal involvement — important surgical landmark
  • Sphenoid sinus and clivus posteriorly
  • Cavernous sinus — always comment on this, as involvement changes surgical approach significantly

A critical practical point:

JNA is one of the most vascular tumours of the head and neck, so biopsy is contraindicated. Diagnosis is made on clinical history and imaging alone. Surgery is preceded by pre-operative angiographic embolization to reduce intraoperative hemorrhage. The radiologist's role is to confirm the diagnosis, map the full extent, and identify feeding vessels for the interventional team.
